items.title: The world's oceans are the hottest on record for June—and El Niño is set to turn up the heat even more
items.summary: The world's oceans are the hottest on record for June, pushing past records set during the 2023–24 El Niño years.

items.title: Scientists may have finally found how Alzheimer's kills brain cells
items.summary: Researchers have identified a previously overlooked mechanism of brain cell death that appears to play a major role in Alzheimer's disease and frontotemporal dementia. The finding could lead to new treatments aimed at slowing neuron loss by interrupting the process before cells are destroyed.

items.title: An island of calm at the violent heart of the galaxy
items.summary: Where would you go to watch a star being born? Probably not the heart of the Milky Way, which is about the most violent neighborhood our galaxy has to offer, a maelstrom of gas churning so fast and so chaotically that you would think nothing could ever settle there long enough to collapse into a sta

items.title: Rats show empathy, according to model
items.summary: A rat first frees a cagemate rat and then shares food with it. Is this animal just as empathetic as humans? In an American study from 2011, researchers observed that rats first freed their fellow rats from a cage and then shared food with them instead of leaving them in the cage and eating alone; th

items.title: Spring songbirds may help pollinate UK trees, with pollen on 89% of studied birds
items.summary: The warblers—those harbingers of spring in the U.K.—have an unappreciated talent of a different sort. Common U.K. birds, including chiffchaffs and blackcaps, are playing a real role in the pollination of plants, according to new research in the Journal of Ecology.

items.title: Deuterium in comets tells interesting tales
items.summary: Comets have played an interesting role in the history of astronomy. Since antiquity, many cultures saw them as omens or spirits, portending good or bad news for kings, queens and emperors. Over the past few hundred years, however, astronomers have studied them intently to understand the science behi

items.title: European cities short on shade as heat waves hit, urban mapping reveals
items.summary: More than four in five homes and workplaces across 25 European cities have less nearby tree canopy than what is needed for meaningful cooling, according to an open-data analysis by an urban greening expert.

items.title: Mars express captures dozens of dust devils in Mars valley
items.summary: Dust devils are a regular feature on Mars. Just like those found on Earth, these mini whirlwinds form when parts of Mars are warmed by the sun, causing air above the surface to swirl upward and carry dust with it. But in Mars' lower gravity (38% that of Earth), these devils grow much larger, re

items.title: A nearby black hole as a window into the early universe
items.summary: An international team led by Stefanie Komossa from the Max Planck Institute for Radio Astronomy (MPIfR) in Bonn has studied a galaxy that has been shining exceptionally brightly in the radio regime for more than eight years. Although it is only 1.8 billion light-years away from us, the central black

items.title: Laughter may date back 15 million years, shared by humans and great apes
items.summary: Humans and great apes have been giggling in similar ways since branching off the evolutionary tree, a new study suggests.

items.title: Japan space probe skims asteroid in test for planetary defense
items.summary: A Japanese space probe performed a flyby of a near-Earth asteroid on Sunday in a test mission for technology that could help protect the planet from space rocks.

items.title: Scattered bronze bells in Chinese lord's 2,600-year-old tomb point to ritual deactivation
items.summary: When archaeologists opened the 2,600-year-old tomb of an ancient Chinese lord, they discovered his magnificent bronze bells had been scattered, their wooden hangings broken. But the most mysterious part of all: This was apparently no accident, with the family of the tomb's owner having chosen t

items.title: Rare 309-million-year-old fossils suggest early tetrapods developed without tadpole phase
items.summary: Scientists have long posited that the earliest water animals to transition to land had amphibious tadpole features, going through a metamorphosis akin to that of today's frogs.

items.title: Mini monitor measures artificial heartbeat
items.summary: An international team, including the University of Tokyo, has created a sensor inspired by the lateral line in fish—their "sixth sense" organ—which measures the pulse of lab-grown 3D heart tissue (cardiac organoids). The device, called a biomechanical well plate, looks like a small white box contain

items.title: Wild yeast discovery enables non-GM brewing of ornithine-enriched craft beer
items.summary: As consumer interest grows in foods and beverages with added nutritional value, brewers are exploring ways to improve fermentation itself rather than relying on post-production additives. Ornithine, a naturally occurring amino acid involved in several biological processes, has attracted attention as

items.title: Spain data on 5.5 million convictions challenges immigration-crime link
items.summary: When analyzing crime, the foreign population typically shows higher rates than the native population. However, crime statistics change significantly when comparing groups of the same age and gender. A detailed data analysis conducted in a study by the Universidad Carlos III de Madrid (UC3M) demonstr

items.title: Controlling magnetic chirality could help memory pack in more data
items.summary: Magnetic storage devices, like a computer's hard disk drive, utilize magnets to represent binary data. However, as these devices are downsized, stray magnetic fields generated by individual magnetic components can interact with neighboring elements to cause operational malfunctions, limiting ho

items.title: VIDEO Otkrivena golema vikinška tekstilna radionica u Danskoj
items.summary: ARHEOLOZI u Danskoj otkrili su golemo vikinško naselje sa 82 tekstilne radionice. Nalaz dokazuje razvijeno gospodarstvo i trgovačke veze do Bliskog istoka, mijenjajući sliku o Vikinzima.

items.title: 'Very dangerous' super typhoon nears US Pacific islands
items.summary: Howling winds and lashing rain battered Guam and the Northern Marianas late Sunday, hours before the projected arrival of a "super typhoon" with force equivalent to a Category 5 hurricane over the U.S. Pacific territories.

items.title: Astronomers find biggest super-puff planets yet that are lighter than cotton candy
items.summary: Astronomers have uncovered a pair of giant planets that are lighter than cotton candy—super-puffs the size of Jupiter.

items.title: Scientists discover the deep sleep circuit that builds muscle, burns fat, and boosts the brain
items.summary: Researchers have identified the brain circuitry that links deep sleep with the release of growth hormone, revealing how the two regulate each other. The newly discovered feedback loop helps explain why poor sleep can interfere with growth, muscle repair, fat metabolism, and brain function. Understan
